current_conditions.site_name: ETOWAH RIVER AT GA 9, NEAR DAWSONVILLE, GA

water_temp_read.read: Water is very warm for trout.

light_pressure_read.read: High pressure and low cloud cover make this fishable but technical.

stocking_read.read: Recent stocking supports eggs, worms, and attractor nymphs.

angler_analysis.expected_trout_behavior: Expect trout to favor the most comfortable feeding lanes while staying close to cover when light is high.
angler_analysis.presentation_style: Prioritize clean drifts, smaller natural patterns, and stocked-trout attractors where appropriate.

fly_reasoning.egg_pattern: Recent stocking makes eggs a logical confidence fly.
fly_reasoning.squirmy_worm: A simple stocked-trout attractor and good dirty-water option.
fly_reasoning.duracell: A strong attractor nymph when fish will move a little but are not fully aggressive.
fly_reasoning.zebra_midge: Small and natural for clear, pressured, technical conditions.
fly_reasoning.pheasant_tail: A reliable natural nymph when trout are feeding but cautious.

caution_notes: Bright/high-pressure conditions may make fish cover-oriented and less forgiving.
Warm water may increase trout stress.

generated_text.quick_read: Trout fishing on the Etowah River is tough due to very warm water and high pressure. Recent stocking adds some optimism using attractor flies and natural patterns.
generated_text.best_window: The best window is day 1 morning because cooler early temperatures and low rain probability provide the most manageable conditions for targeting stocked trout using careful, less aggressive presentations.
generated_text.current_conditions: Current water temperature is 81.5°F, flow is 108 cfs, gage height is 3.77 ft, and turbidity is 7.8 NTU, indicating warm and moderately clear freestone flow.
generated_text.weather_setup: Sky cover averages 22% with high pressure at 30.12 inHg and calm winds around 1.6 mph. Rain probability is low at 14% with no significant rainfall expected.
generated_text.what_this_means: Trout will seek cold-water refuges, oxygenated pockets, shade, deeper runs, and cover like undercut banks. Use smaller natural flies and stocked-trout attractors such as egg patterns, squirmy worms, and Duracell nymphs with clean drifts and less aggressive presentations. Exercise caution as bright, high-pressure, and warm conditions increase trout stress and promote cover-oriented behavior.
